brief_summary: Two-column do/don't map. The do column (amber checkmarks) lists changing one axis at a time, keeping the old board as a fallback, and committing to the firmware before the layout. The don't column (warm-red x marks) lists stacking every axis on a first board, selling the old board immediately, and judging week one. Every item is drawn directly from the article's 'Common pitfalls' section.
data_sources: split-ergo-buyers-guide.mdx § 'Common pitfalls' — 'The most common mistake is stacking all four axes on a first purchase — split, column-stagger, a fully ortho thumb cluster, and an aggressive tent angle'
split-ergo-buyers-guide.mdx § 'Common pitfalls' — 'The third is selling the old board too early'
split-ergo-buyers-guide.mdx § 'How to actually choose' — 'Keeping the old board plugged in and available during that window... removes the pressure that turns a bad week into an abandoned experiment'

split-ergo-buyers-guide.mdx § 'Common pitfalls' — 'using the stock layout for the first few weeks before customizing layers is not a compromise, it's the fastest path to fluency' (firmware-before-layout ordering)
warnings: 'Judge week one' condenses the article's discussion of the multi-week relearning curve into a single caution label; the underlying claim (don't evaluate the transition after only a few days) is supported by the 'budget the relearning weeks' step but not stated verbatim.
